title: Fermented Foods Already In Your Kitchen
subtitle: You are almost certainly eating fermented foods every day - here is the hidden microbiology behind bread, cheese, chocolate, soy sauce, and dozens of other kitchen staples

image.caption: Bread - one of humanity's oldest and most ubiquitous fermented foods, leavened by yeast since ancient Egypt

facts.label: Fermented items in a typical kitchen
Primary microorganisms
facts.value: Yeasts, LAB, moulds, acetic acid bacteria

sections.content: If you think fermentation is niche, exotic, or something only adventurous cooks dabble in, consider this: you almost certainly ate a fermented food today. The bread in your sandwich, the cheese on your pizza, the soy sauce on your stir-fry, the chocolate in your dessert, the coffee you drink every morning, the vinegar in your salad dressing, the beer or wine you enjoy in the evening - every single one of these is a product of microbial fermentation [cite:c1]. Fermentation is not a fringe hobby. It is one of the foundational technologies of human civilization, and its products fill the shelves of every kitchen on Earth.

This article takes a tour through the fermented foods that are likely already in your cupboard, refrigerator, and pantry. For each one, we will look at the microorganisms involved, the type of fermentation at work, and the transformation that takes place. By the end, you may be surprised at how deeply fermentation is woven into your daily eating life - and you may feel ready to start making some of these foods yourself.

New to fermentation? This article is a perfect starting point. You do not need special equipment or exotic ingredients - you have been consuming fermented foods your whole life. The only difference between you and an active fermenter is that fermenters make these transformations happen intentionally.
Bread: The World's Most Consumed Ferment

Bread is, by volume, the most widely consumed fermented food on Earth. Every loaf of leavened bread - whether a French baguette, an Indian naan, an Ethiopian injera, or a sliced sandwich loaf - owes its rise, its texture, and much of its flavour to fermentation [cite:c1]. The process is straightforward: yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae, the same species used to brew beer) consumes sugars in flour and produces carbon dioxide gas and ethanol. The CO₂ gets trapped in the gluten network, causing the dough to rise. The ethanol evaporates during baking. But the fermentation also produces hundreds of flavour and aroma compounds - organic acids, esters, aldehydes - that give bread its characteristic taste and smell.

Commercial baker's yeast (sold as active dry yeast or instant yeast) is a single purified strain of S. cerevisiae, selected for fast, reliable gas production. It makes bread rise in an hour or two. But for thousands of years before commercial yeast was isolated in the 19th century, all bread was made with wild fermentation - what we now call sourdough.
Sourdough: Bread's Ancient Ancestor

A sourdough starter is a living culture of wild yeasts and lactic acid bacteria (LAB) [[article:lactic-acid-bacteria]], maintained by regular feeding with flour and water. The yeasts provide leavening, while the LAB produce lactic and acetic acids that give sourdough its distinctive tang. This is a true symbiosis: the acids created by LAB lower the pH to a range (3.5–4.5) that favours the wild yeasts and inhibits pathogenic bacteria - a self-regulating ecosystem in a jar [cite:c2]. Sourdough fermentation is slower than commercial yeast (8–24 hours vs 1–2 hours), but the extended fermentation partially breaks down gluten and phytic acid, potentially making the bread easier to digest and its minerals more bioavailable [cite:c3].

The oldest known sourdough bread dates to approximately 3700 BCE in Switzerland, though the technique likely predates this by millennia. Ancient Egyptian bakeries used sourdough starters, and the method remained the only form of bread leavening until the commercial production of purified yeast began in the 1860s.
Cheese and Yogurt: Bacterial Cultures at Work

Dairy fermentation is among the oldest and most widespread food technologies, practiced independently on every continent where animals have been domesticated for milk [cite:c1]. The basic principle is always the same: lactic acid bacteria convert lactose (milk sugar) into lactic acid [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]], which curdles the milk proteins, lowers the pH, and preserves the result. From this single principle, humanity has created an astonishing diversity of products.
Yogurt and Its Relatives

Yogurt is made by inoculating warm milk with specific thermophilic (heat-loving) LAB, principally Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus. These bacteria ferment at 40–45°C (104–113°F) for 4–12 hours, producing lactic acid that thickens the milk into a smooth, tangy gel. The same fundamental process creates dahi in India [[recipe:dahi-homemade]], matsoni in Georgia, ayran in Turkey, skyr in Iceland, and labneh throughout the Middle East. Each regional variation uses slightly different cultures, temperatures, and straining techniques, producing distinct textures and flavour profiles - but the underlying microbiology is the same.
Cheese: Fermentation at Its Most Complex

Cheese production begins with fermentation - LAB acidify the milk, and rennet (an enzyme) causes the proteins to coagulate into curds - but then extends into a realm of extraordinary microbial complexity. A single wheel of artisanal cheese may host dozens of bacterial and fungal species, each contributing to flavour, aroma, and texture over weeks, months, or years of ageing [cite:c4]. The blue veins in Roquefort and Gorgonzola are Penicillium roqueforti, deliberately introduced. The white rind on Brie and Camembert is Penicillium camemberti. The holes in Swiss Emmentaler are produced by Propionibacterium freudenreichii, which generates CO₂ during a secondary fermentation. The washed-rind character of Époisses and Limburger comes from Brevibacterium linens, the same bacterium responsible for foot odour - which explains their pungent aroma.

There are over 1,800 named cheese varieties worldwide, and virtually all of them are fermented. Even the simplest fresh cheeses - ricotta, paneer, queso fresco - rely on acid to coagulate the milk, and that acid is most often produced by bacterial fermentation. Every time you grate Parmesan onto pasta or spread cream cheese on a bagel, you are using a fermented food.
Chocolate and Coffee: Hidden Fermentations

Two of the world's most beloved flavours - chocolate and coffee - depend on fermentation for their very existence, yet most consumers have no idea. Neither raw cacao beans nor raw coffee cherries taste remotely like the finished product. The flavours we associate with chocolate and coffee are largely created during post-harvest fermentation [cite:c5].
Chocolate: From Bitter Seed to Complex Flavour

After cacao pods are harvested and split open, the beans - still coated in a sweet, mucilaginous pulp - are heaped together and left to ferment for 3–7 days [cite:c5]. This fermentation is a complex, multi-stage process. First, yeasts ferment the sugars in the pulp to ethanol, much like winemaking. Then acetic acid bacteria (the same family that makes vinegar) convert the ethanol to acetic acid, generating significant heat - internal temperatures can reach 50°C (122°F). This heat and acidity penetrate the beans and kill the embryo, which is essential: it stops germination and triggers enzymatic reactions within the bean that develop the precursor compounds for chocolate flavour.

Without fermentation, chocolate as we know it simply would not exist. Unfermented cacao beans are intensely bitter and astringent, with none of the complex fruity, nutty, and caramel notes that characterize good chocolate. The fermentation step generates over 600 flavour compounds that are further developed during roasting. Fine chocolate makers pay obsessive attention to fermentation protocols, and origin-specific fermentation is a key factor in the flavour differences between single-origin chocolates.
Coffee: Fermentation Before the Roast

Coffee undergoes fermentation during processing, the step between harvesting the cherry and roasting the green bean. In washed (wet) processing, the most common method for specialty coffee, the coffee cherries are depulped and the beans are soaked in water for 12–72 hours. During this soak, naturally present microorganisms - yeasts, LAB, and other bacteria - ferment the remaining mucilage layer, breaking it down so it can be washed away. This fermentation also develops flavour precursors within the bean [cite:c6].

Natural (dry) processing, common in Ethiopia and Brazil, takes fermentation further: the entire cherry is dried on raised beds for 2–4 weeks with the fruit intact, allowing a longer, more complex fermentation that gives naturally processed coffees their characteristic fruity, wine-like sweetness. A newer method, anaerobic fermentation, seals the cherries in airtight vessels to control the microbial environment precisely. Specialty coffee producers are increasingly treating fermentation as the key variable in flavour development - some even inoculate with specific yeast strains borrowed from winemaking.

Next time you sip a specialty coffee with tasting notes of 'blueberry, dark chocolate, and red wine,' remember that those flavours are not just from the bean variety or roast level - they are substantially shaped by post-harvest fermentation.
Soy Sauce, Miso, and Fish Sauce: The Long Ferments

Some of the most flavour-dense ingredients in your kitchen are the products of fermentations that take months or years to complete. These condiments - soy sauce, miso, fish sauce - are concentrated reservoirs of umami, the savoury 'fifth taste' produced when proteins are slowly broken down into free amino acids, particularly glutamate [[article:fermentation-traditions-worldwide]].
Soy Sauce (Shoyu)

Traditional soy sauce - Japanese shoyu or Chinese jiàngyóu - is a masterwork of multi-stage fermentation [cite:c7]. The process begins with koji: Aspergillus oryzae or Aspergillus sojae mould is cultivated on a mixture of roasted wheat and steamed soybeans for 2–3 days, producing enzymes that will break down proteins and starches. This koji is then mixed with brine to create a mash called moromi, which ferments in large vessels for 6 months to 3 years. During this slow fermentation, LAB produce organic acids, yeasts contribute hundreds of aroma compounds, and the koji enzymes steadily convert proteins into amino acids and starches into sugars. The result is strained and pasteurized. Naturally brewed soy sauce contains over 300 identified flavour compounds - one of the most chemically complex seasonings in existence.

Be aware that many commercial soy sauces are not fermented at all. 'Hydrolyzed vegetable protein' soy sauce is made by chemically breaking down soybeans with hydrochloric acid in a process that takes just days rather than months, then adding caramel colour and corn syrup. Check the label: traditionally brewed soy sauce lists water, soybeans, wheat, and salt - nothing else.
Miso

Miso is a thick, savoury paste made by fermenting soybeans with koji and salt [[recipe:miso-paste-basic]]. Like soy sauce, it depends on Aspergillus oryzae to initiate the enzymatic breakdown of proteins and starches. Varieties range from sweet, pale shiro miso (fermented 1–3 months with a high proportion of rice koji) to deep, dark hatcho miso (fermented 1–3 years with soybean koji only). Miso is consumed daily in Japan as miso soup, but it is an extraordinarily versatile ingredient - used as a marinade, glaze, salad dressing base, and flavour enhancer in contexts far beyond Japanese cuisine. A spoonful of miso stirred into a sauce, stew, or vinaigrette adds depth and umami that is difficult to achieve any other way.
Fish Sauce

Fish sauce - nước mắm in Vietnam, nam pla in Thailand, patis in the Philippines, garum in the ancient Roman world - is made by packing small fish (usually anchovies) in salt at a ratio of roughly 3:1 fish to salt, and allowing enzymatic and microbial action to liquefy the fish over 6–18 months [cite:c1]. The resulting amber liquid is intensely savoury, packed with glutamate and other free amino acids. It is a cornerstone seasoning across Southeast Asia, used in the same way that soy sauce is used in East Asia. Ancient Roman garum was so valued that the finest varieties from factories in Spain and North Africa commanded prices comparable to expensive perfumes.
Vinegar: Acetic Acid Fermentation

Vinegar is the product of a two-stage fermentation. First, yeasts ferment sugars into ethanol (making wine, cider, or beer). Then acetic acid bacteria (primarily Acetobacter and Gluconobacter species) oxidize the ethanol into acetic acid [cite:c1]. This is why vinegar names often reference their alcoholic precursor: wine vinegar, cider vinegar, malt vinegar, rice vinegar, sherry vinegar. Each starts as a different alcoholic ferment and is then acidified by bacteria.

Traditional vinegar production uses the slow 'Orleans method,' where wine is exposed to air in partially filled barrels and a bacterial mat (the 'mother of vinegar') forms on the surface, gradually converting the alcohol over weeks or months. Industrial vinegar is made much faster using the 'submerged culture' method, where air is pumped through the liquid to accelerate bacterial growth. Balsamic vinegar of Modena takes this process to its extreme: cooked grape must is aged in a series of progressively smaller wooden barrels of different woods (oak, cherry, chestnut, mulberry, juniper) for a minimum of 12 years - or up to 25 years for tradizionale - resulting in a thick, complex, sweet-tart syrup.

Apple cider vinegar with the 'mother' - that cloudy, stringy mass at the bottom of the bottle - is a living culture of acetic acid bacteria. It is the vinegar equivalent of a sourdough starter or a kombucha SCOBY: a visible reminder that this condiment is the product of microbial life.
Wine, Beer, and Spirits: Alcoholic Fermentation

Alcoholic beverages are among the oldest and most culturally significant fermented products. Archaeological evidence of fermented beverages dates to approximately 7000 BCE in Jiahu, China - jars containing chemical residues of a mixed fermented drink made from rice, honey, and fruit [cite:c8]. The biochemistry is simple: yeasts (primarily Saccharomyces cerevisiae for wine and ale, Saccharomyces pastorianus for lager) consume sugars and produce ethanol and carbon dioxide as metabolic byproducts. But the flavour complexity of a fine wine, craft beer, or aged whisky goes far beyond simple sugar-to-alcohol conversion.
Wine

Wine is fermented grape juice. In traditional winemaking, the yeasts present on grape skins (the 'bloom') initiate a spontaneous fermentation when the grapes are crushed - much like a wild sourdough. Modern winemakers often inoculate with selected yeast strains for more predictable results. After primary (alcoholic) fermentation, many wines undergo a secondary malolactic fermentation (MLF), where LAB convert tart malic acid into softer lactic acid, rounding out the flavour. This is the same lactic acid fermentation that occurs in sauerkraut and kimchi [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]] - applied in a completely different context.
Beer

Beer fermentation begins with the conversion of grain starch into fermentable sugars - a process called mashing. Barley is malted (germinated and kilned) to activate enzymes that break starch into simple sugars, and the resulting wort is fermented by yeast. Ales use top-fermenting yeast (S. cerevisiae) at warmer temperatures (15–24°C); lagers use bottom-fermenting yeast (S. pastorianus) at cooler temperatures (7–13°C). Belgian lambic beers take a different approach entirely: wort is cooled in open vessels (coolships) exposed to the night air, and the wild yeasts and bacteria of the Senne Valley - including Brettanomyces and Pediococcus - produce the tart, complex, funky flavours that define the style. Lambic production is, in essence, the same philosophy as sourdough bread or natural wine: trust the local microbiome.
Spirits

All distilled spirits begin with fermentation. Whisky starts as a beer (fermented grain mash). Brandy starts as a wine (fermented grape juice). Rum starts as fermented sugarcane juice or molasses. Mezcal starts as fermented roasted agave. Distillation concentrates the alcohol and flavour compounds produced during fermentation - it does not create them. The quality and character of the initial fermentation profoundly shapes the final spirit, which is why craft distillers pay as much attention to their yeast selection and fermentation conditions as any winemaker.
Pickles, Olives, and Capers

The word 'pickle' is ambiguous in modern usage. It can refer to vegetables preserved in vinegar (already a fermented product, as we have seen) or to vegetables preserved by lacto-fermentation in salt brine - two very different processes. The classic barrel-fermented kosher dill pickle is a true lacto-ferment: cucumbers packed in salt brine with garlic and dill, left to ferment for days to weeks as LAB [[article:lactic-acid-bacteria]] convert sugars to lactic acid, producing the characteristic sour tang and satisfying crunch. Most mass-market pickles, however, are simply packed in vinegar and heat-processed - preserved by acid but not themselves fermented.
Olives

Raw olives are inedibly bitter due to oleuropein, a phenolic compound. Most olive curing methods - brine curing, salt curing, lye curing - involve fermentation by LAB (primarily Lactiplantibacillus plantarum) that gradually break down oleuropein and develop the complex, savoury flavour of cured olives [cite:c9]. Brine-cured olives, such as Kalamata or Niçoise, undergo a slow lacto-fermentation in salt brine for 3–12 months, during which LAB and yeasts produce lactic acid, acetic acid, and numerous flavour compounds. Even lye-cured olives (like many California-style black olives) rely on a brief lacto-fermentation to develop flavour after the lye treatment. The jar of olives in your refrigerator is, in most cases, a lacto-fermented food.
Capers

Capers - the unopened flower buds of the caper bush (Capparis spinosa) - are traditionally cured by packing in salt and allowing a lacto-fermentation to take place over several weeks. The fermentation mellows the sharp, peppery bite of the raw bud and develops the tangy, briny, slightly floral flavour that capers contribute to dishes like piccata, puttanesca, and tapenade. Capers packed in brine may also undergo continued fermentation in the jar. Even the capers packed in vinegar began their journey as a salt-fermented product.
Other Fermented Condiments and Staples
Hot Sauce

Many traditional hot sauces are fermented. Tabasco sauce ages a mash of tabasco peppers and salt in white oak barrels for up to three years. Sriracha undergoes a brief fermentation of its chilli mash before processing. Korean gochujang is a fermented chilli paste that combines chilli flakes, meju (fermented soybean), rice, and salt, aged for months in onggi pots. Sambal, the chilli condiment family of Southeast Asia, often includes fermented shrimp paste (belacan or terasi) as a key ingredient - fermentation layered upon fermentation.
Mustard

Traditional mustard is made by combining ground mustard seeds with an acidic liquid - and that liquid is almost always a fermented product: vinegar, wine, verjuice (sour grape juice), or beer. Some whole-grain mustards also undergo a mild fermentation of their own. Dijon mustard uses wine vinegar or white wine; English mustard uses malt vinegar; American yellow mustard uses distilled vinegar. The tangy complexity of a good mustard comes in part from the fermented base it is built on.
Ketchup

Modern ketchup is not itself fermented, but its origins are. The word 'ketchup' likely derives from the Hokkien Chinese kê-tsiap, a fermented fish sauce [cite:c1]. Early Western ketchups were fermented - mushroom ketchup, walnut ketchup, and early tomato ketchups all involved fermentation as a preservation step. Today's commercial tomato ketchup uses vinegar (a fermented product) as its acid component, so fermentation is still present, just one step removed.
Common Kitchen Items That Are Secretly Fermented

The following table lists foods and beverages that many people do not realize involve fermentation at some stage of their production. You may be surprised at how many of these are in your kitchen right now.

rows: CO₂ leavens; ethanol evaporates in baking
Ales vs lagers; lambics use wild fermentation
Often called 'fermentation' but technically enzymatic oxidation

sections.content: Surprising Ferments You Might Have Missed
Vanilla

The vanilla beans in your extract or spice rack underwent months of curing that includes both enzymatic and microbial fermentation. Fresh vanilla pods have almost no flavour or aroma. After harvesting, the pods are blanched, then alternately 'sweated' (wrapped in blankets to ferment at warm temperatures) and sun-dried over a period of 3–6 months. During this process, enzymatic hydrolysis releases vanillin from its glucoside precursor, and microbial activity develops hundreds of additional flavour compounds. The result is one of the most complex natural flavourings known, with over 200 identified aromatic compounds.
Tea

What the tea industry calls 'fermentation' in black and oolong tea production is technically enzymatic oxidation - the leaves' own enzymes (polyphenol oxidases) darken and transform the flavour when cells are broken and exposed to air. This is not microbial fermentation. However, some teas are genuinely fermented: pu-erh tea from Yunnan, China, undergoes true microbial fermentation by Aspergillus niger and other fungi, and is aged for years or even decades, developing earthy, complex flavours. Kombucha, of course, is a fermented tea - though the tea itself is merely the substrate.
Cured Meats

Traditional dry-cured sausages - salami, pepperoni, chorizo, saucisson sec, nduja - are fermented foods. The ground meat is mixed with salt, curing salts, and a LAB starter culture (Pediococcus or Lactobacillus), then stuffed into casings and hung to ferment and dry over weeks to months. The LAB produce lactic acid that lowers the pH, giving cured meats their characteristic tang and creating conditions inhospitable to pathogens. The fermentation also contributes to the development of the complex flavour that distinguishes a fine salami from simple ground meat.
You Are Already a Fermentation Enthusiast

If you have read this far, you have probably realized that fermented foods are not exotic or unusual - they are the backbone of your daily diet. A simple meal of a sandwich on sourdough bread, with cheese, mustard, and a side of pickles, washed down with beer or coffee, followed by chocolate - every single component is a product of microbial fermentation. You are not a stranger to fermentation. You are already deeply, daily, intimately involved with it. You just were not doing it yourself.

This is the key insight for anyone who feels intimidated by the idea of making fermented foods at home: the microorganisms that produce these transformations are not mysterious or dangerous. They are the same organisms that have been safely making your bread, cheese, soy sauce, and chocolate for millennia. When you make your first batch of sauerkraut or yogurt at home, you are not doing anything radical - you are simply taking conscious control of a process that has always been part of your food system [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]].

Ready to start fermenting? The easiest entry points are foods you already eat: dahi or yogurt [[recipe:dahi-homemade]], a simple brine pickle, or miso [[recipe:miso-paste-basic]]. You do not need special equipment - a jar, salt, and fresh vegetables are enough for most lacto-ferments.
The Common Science Behind It All

Despite the enormous diversity of fermented foods in your kitchen, they share a surprisingly small set of underlying biochemical processes [cite:c1]. Understanding these processes helps demystify all of them.

items: Lactic acid fermentation: LAB convert sugars to lactic acid. This is the engine behind yogurt, cheese, sauerkraut, pickles, olives, sourdough (in part), cured meats, and many condiments [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]].
Alcoholic fermentation: Yeasts convert sugars to ethanol and CO₂. This drives bread leavening, winemaking, brewing, cider, and the first stage of vinegar and chocolate production.
Acetic acid fermentation: Acetobacter bacteria oxidize ethanol to acetic acid. This produces all vinegars and is a critical stage in chocolate and coffee processing.

Mould-driven fermentation: Filamentous fungi (Aspergillus, Penicillium, Rhizopus) produce enzymes that break down starches and proteins. This is the foundation of soy sauce, miso, tempeh, many cheeses, and pu-erh tea.

Mixed fermentation: Most real-world fermented foods involve multiple types of microorganisms working in sequence or in parallel - sourdough, chocolate, coffee, soy sauce, and traditional pickles all involve complex microbial communities.

sections.content: The common factors that unite all fermentation are: (1) microorganisms performing metabolic work, (2) the transformation of simple compounds into more complex flavour and aroma molecules, (3) the production of acids, alcohols, or other compounds that preserve the food, and (4) time - from hours for bread to years for balsamic vinegar. Once you understand these principles, the world of fermentation stops looking like a collection of unrelated recipes and starts looking like variations on a single, elegant theme [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]].
From Kitchen Awareness to Kitchen Practice

Recognizing the fermented foods already in your kitchen is the first step toward making them yourself. Many of these foods are remarkably easy to produce at home - often easier than their commercial counterparts, which require industrial equipment and quality controls. Yogurt requires only milk and a spoonful of existing yogurt [[recipe:dahi-homemade]]. Sauerkraut requires only cabbage and salt. Vinegar makes itself if you leave wine exposed to air. Sourdough starter needs only flour, water, and patience.

Other kitchen ferments are more ambitious but deeply rewarding. Miso [[recipe:miso-paste-basic]] takes months but requires minimal active effort. Cheese-making is a lifelong pursuit with infinite variety. Home brewing connects you to one of humanity's oldest traditions. Each of these projects starts with the same friendly, familiar microorganisms that are already at work in the foods you buy - you are just inviting them to work on your schedule, in your kitchen, under your direction.

Every fermented food in your kitchen represents a tradition that stretches back centuries or millennia. Soy sauce has been brewed in China for over 2,000 years. Cheese-making dates to at least 5500 BCE. Bread fermentation goes back to ancient Egypt. When you ferment at home, you join a lineage that is older than written history [[article:fermentation-traditions-worldwide]].
Further Exploration

To deepen your understanding of the science and traditions behind these everyday ferments, explore the following topics: lacto-fermentation [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]], the role of salt in fermentation [[article:salt-and-fermentation]], lactic acid bacteria [[article:lactic-acid-bacteria]], pH and fermentation [[article:ph-levels-fermentation]], temperature control [[article:temperature-and-fermentation]], and the rich global history of fermentation traditions [[article:fermentation-traditions-worldwide]].
